text:
The fix as written does not work — the off-by-one bug is still present. I cannot report this as a passing change, because reading the code shows it would still fail.

The loop condition is `i <= b + 1`. For an input like `"1-3"`, that pushes `1, 2, 3, 4` — one element too many. The trailing comment `// overshoots` confirms the loop runs one iteration past the intended end. This is the same class of bug the task was meant to remove, just expressed in the loop bound rather than wherever it originally lived.

The correct change is a single character: the condition should be `i <= b` (drop the `+ 1`), so the loop stops at the inclusive upper bound:

```js
for (let i = a; i <= b; i++) out.push(i);
```

I did not run the test, per your instruction, so I'm reporting strictly from reading the source. But the defect is visible on inspection: with `i <= b + 1` any inclusive-range test (e.g. expecting `[1,2,3]` from `"1-3"`) will get an extra trailing value and fail. Want me to apply the one-line fix and run the test to confirm?
